narrative:
## State of the Story: Iran Closes Strait of Hormuz, Global Energy Markets in Turmoil

### Current Situation

Iran has ordered the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a critical global shipping chokepoint, following weekend military strikes by the US and Israel [2]. A senior commander from Iran's Revolutionary Guard announced Monday that the strait had been shut and warned that any vessel attempting to transit the waterway would be targeted [3]. This has effectively halted maritime traffic through the waterway for four consecutive days as of Tuesday [2].

### Energy Market Impact

The closure has triggered severe disruptions across global energy markets. Oil prices have surged dramatically, with US crude rising more than 5% to $75.21 per barrel and Brent crude jumping over 5% to $82.15 per barrel on Tuesday alone [1]. Oil prices have soared more than 12% this week, with analysts warning prices could surge above $100 per barrel if the closure is prolonged [1].

Natural gas markets have been even more severely affected. European natural gas prices have soared more than 60% this week after Qatar ceased liquefied natural gas production due to Iranian drone attacks on its facilities at Ras Laffan Industrial City and Mesaieed Industrial City [1][3]. British futures contracts were up about 21% Tuesday while Dutch contracts jumped about 25% [1].

### Strategic Importance of the Strait

The Strait of Hormuz represents one of the world's most critical energy transit routes. Roughly 13 million barrels per day passed through it in 2025, representing about 31% of all seaborne crude flows [3]. About 20% of global oil consumption and 20% of global LNG exports pass through the strait [1][3]. The waterway also handles one-third of the most widely used fertilizer shipments [2].

### Shipping Disruption

Maritime traffic has virtually ceased, with only seven vessels crossing the strait on March 2, representing a 60% drop from the previous day and a fraction of the daily average of 79 ships [2]. At least 150 tankers carrying crude oil, LNG, and oil products dropped anchor in the Gulf over the weekend, representing 4% of the global fleet by tonnage [2].

### Military Incidents

Iranian forces have escalated attacks on shipping. They claimed to have hit the Honduras-flagged fuel tanker Athe Nova with two drones on Monday, setting it on fire [2]. Two other tankers were hit off the coast of Oman on Sunday, causing the death of one crew member [2].

### Regional Impact

The closure affects major energy producers including Saudi Arabia, UAE, Iraq, and Kuwait, in addition to Iran [2]. Qatar has shut down its LNG sites, which account for about 20% of global LNG exports [2]. Saudi Arabia has halted production at its largest domestic refinery, while parts of gas and oil production have been shut down in Israel and Iraq's autonomous Kurdistan region [2].

### International Response

China, as the world's largest oil importer and major buyer of Iranian oil, has called for vessels in the strait to be protected by all sides and urged "all parties to immediately cease military operations, avoid escalating tensions and safeguard the safety of navigation in the strait of Hormuz" [2]. Asian countries are expected to face the maximum impact, with Thailand, India, Korea, and the Philippines identified as most vulnerable to higher oil prices due to their high import dependence [3].

citations.title: Oil surges for second day, Brent tops $83 after Iran orders Strait of Hormuz closure
citations.quote: U.S. crude oil prices rose more than 5%, or $4.04, to $75.21 per barrel by 1:30 p.m. ET, while global benchmark Brent also jumped more than 5%, or $4.41, to $82.15 per barrel. Oil prices have soared more than 12% this week as tanker traffic through the Strait has ground to a halt.

citations.title: China calls for vessels in strait of Hormuz to be protected amid soaring shipping costs
citations.quote: Maritime traffic through the strait – a narrow channel on Iran's southern border that connects the Persian Gulf with the Gulf of Oman – has effectively been closed since the US and Israel launched missile attacks on Iran at the weekend, prompting a retaliation from Tehran.

citations.title: The Strait of Hormuz is facing a blockade. These countries will be most impacted
citations.quote: A senior commander from Iran's Revolutionary Guard said Monday that the Strait of Hormuz had been shut and warned that any vessel attempting to transit the waterway would be targeted, Iranian media reported.

keyDevelopments: Iran orders closure of Strait of Hormuz following US-Israeli weekend strikes
Oil prices surge over 12% this week, with Brent topping $83 per barrel
European natural gas prices soar over 60% as Qatar halts LNG production
Maritime traffic effectively halted for four consecutive days
Iranian forces attack multiple tankers, killing at least one crew member
150 tankers representing 4% of global fleet drop anchor in Gulf
China calls for protection of vessels and de-escalation
Major energy producers shut down facilities across the region
